text:
From: Priya Shah <ops@fjordcommerce.com>
To: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Cc: Amit Rao <amit@fjordcommerce.com>
Date: 2028-07-26T08:14:22-07:00
Subject: Unexpected 429s + huge tail on checkout pipeline

Jordan —

We started seeing a surge of 429s and multi-second tail completions on our checkout personalization calls around 07:40 UTC. Traffic pattern looks normal on our edge but Redwood API replies are taking 5–12s on the 95–99th percentile and 2% of requests are 429-ing. This is impacting user checkout completion.

Attached: a short request sample and a handful of traces (we redacted PII). Can your team confirm whether this is a routing/concurrency cap or an internal queueing/backpressure event on your side?

We're getting pressure from ops and product — need a quick status and a mitigation path.

Thanks,
Priya
Priya Shah
Head of Platform Reliability, FjordCommerce

Diego —

Thanks for taking this call earlier. As part of BrightBank's procurement security review we...
text:
From: Anita Rao <anita.rao@brightbank.com>
To: Diego Fernandez <diego@redwood.ai>
Cc: security@brightbank.com
Date: Tue, 18 Jul 2028 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: RFI — default log retention: requests vs generated content

Diego —

Thanks for taking this call earlier. As part of BrightBank's procurement security review we need a concise, written answer to how Redwood handles default log retention for interactive requests. Specifically: what you store by default for (1) raw user requests/prompts, (2) model-generated outputs/responses, and (3) streaming/intermediate tokens (KV cache, partial outputs). We also need to understand default retention windows, whether any of this is persisted to long-term telemetry stores, and the erasure path if we request deletion.

Could you provide an example statement we can include in our SOC questionnaire and any supporting artifact (e.g., a one-page matrix)...

Subject: Adaptive Pack — tier swap options & stepdown guardrails

Hi Eli —

Thanks again for the time yesterday. Per our conversation I sketched three practical options for Courtyard’s move from Compact to Adaptive Pack with a fallback path if load dips below expected thresholds. Attached is a short PDF that summarizes the tradeoffs; calling out the parts most relevant to you below so it’s quick to scan.

Quick TL;DR:
- Option A (Gradual swap): 3-month ramp to full Adaptive Pack, prorated credits if throughput drops >30% month-over-month. Lowest up-front commitment.
- Option B (Pilot commit): 6-month pilot with guaranteed per-token floor and a mid-term pricing review; includes 2 p...
